symbols: stone sarcophagus — the tomb hewn from rock provided by Joseph of Arimathea, receiving the body of God incarnate
linen burial cloth — the clean linen shroud in which Christ was wrapped, a sign of purity and sacrificial completion
kneeling mourning woman — devotion and grief at the foot of the dead Christ, embodying the Church's sorrow
anointing vessels — the spices and ointments prepared for burial, fulfilling Jewish custom and prefiguring resurrection hope
bare trees and rocky landscape — the desolation of creation at the death of its Creator
description: A horizontal predella panel depicting the entombment of Christ. At center, the pale, lifeless body of Jesus is being lowered into a stone sarcophagus by several figures. Multiple men with halos support the body from either side — one grasping under the arms, others holding the legs and midsection. The body is partially wrapped in white linen. At the far right, a woman kneels in deep grief, her head bowed and covered. At the far left, another figure looks on. The background shows a spare, rocky landscape with bare trees. In the left background, additional small figures can be seen.
theological_description: The Entombment of Christ depicts the solemn moment described in all four Gospels when the body of Jesus was removed from the cross and placed in a new tomb. Joseph of Arimathea, a secret disciple and member of the Sanhedrin, boldly went to Pilate and asked for the body (Mark 15:43). Together with Nicodemus, who brought a mixture of myrrh and aloes (John 19:39), they wrapped the body in clean linen and laid it in Joseph's own new tomb hewn from rock (Matthew 27:59-60). This moment stands at the theological hinge between the completed sacrifice of the cross and the coming triumph of the Resurrection. Christ's burial is not merely historical detail but a creedal affirmation — 'He was buried' (1 Corinthians 15:4) — confirming the reality of his death and therefore the reality of his rising. The stone tomb becomes a womb of new creation: for three days, as Jonah was in the belly of the great fish, so the Son of Man was in the heart of the earth (Matthew 12:40). The grieving women fulfill the prophetic picture of Zechariah 12:10, 'They will look on me, the one they have pierced, and they will mourn.' Yet within this scene of desolation lies hidden the seed of the greatest reversal in history: the tomb that receives a corpse will yield the Risen Lord.

scripture_refs.reason: John's account of Joseph of Arimathea and Nicodemus taking the body, wrapping it in linen with spices, and placing it in a new garden tomb
Matthew's account of Joseph wrapping the body in clean linen and laying it in his own new tomb hewn from rock
Mark's account of the burial, noting that Mary Magdalene and Mary the mother of Joses watched where the body was laid
Paul's creedal formula affirming 'he was buried' as essential to the gospel proclamation
Jesus's prediction that as Jonah was three days in the fish, so the Son of Man would be three days in the earth
